files.SKILL.md:
# Steady State

The work that lasts comes from three habits held consistently:
read the right things, change the minimum, record the pattern.

---

## What to read, every time

| Source | What it tells you | When to read it |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| Environment documentation | Rules of the room, available tools | First, before anything else |
| Task description | The deliverable, verbatim | Second, word for word |
| Persistent notes | Prior findings, open problems | Third, before forming a plan |
| Visible tests | The contract in executable form | Before touching source |

Names — of tests, functions, files, parameters — encode what
the original author decided was worth preserving. Read them.

Prior notes are strong hints, not authority. Verify the specific
detail against live state before relying on it. When live
evidence contradicts a stored entry, live evidence wins.

---

## How to act

The task description names the deliverable, its location, and
its format. These are not defaults — reproduce them exactly.
A spec rewritten in your own words is a spec violated.

Touch only what the task names. Adjacent code that already
works is out of scope. Prefer the narrowest change that
satisfies every constraint at once.

If prior notes name the root cause and fix location, go there
first. Read only enough to confirm the context still holds.
Reuse the lesson; re-verify the facts.

---

## Properties that are always required

These are not optional. Walk each before declaring done.

**Stability.** If this ran for weeks, does anything grow
without bound — map entries, queue depth, handle count? Growth
without a removal path is a defect the test suite cannot catch.

**Concurrency.** The read-decide-write sequence over shared
state must be atomic. Two threads interleaving through any gap
in that sequence can corrupt the result. Wrap the triple.

**Clock integrity.** Wall clocks can be stepped by external
sources. Use the monotonic clock for any elapsed-time reasoning.
One line. No exceptions.

**Boundary behaviour.** Zero, one, the type maximum, and the
negative if the type permits. The code must hold there or fail
loudly. Quiet wrongness at the edge is the worst failure mode.

**Invariant preservation.** A change that fixes one property
while regressing another is not a fix. Establish every relevant
property at the same time.

---

## Commit sequence

1. Set git identity — a missing identity silently blocks commits.
2. Branch from the most recent prior task branch, not the default.
3. Commit the first working state before the full suite runs.
   A committed partial result survives a timeout; uncommitted
   complete work does not.
4. Stage by exact path. Short messages. No amend or rebase.

---

## Verification

Run whatever check the environment provides. Read the full
output. Re-read what you changed afterward — a success response
confirms the call landed, not that the state is correct.

If the check fails again after a code change, the diagnosis was
wrong. Stop. Re-read. Re-locate the fault.

---

## What to record

| Record | Skip |
|--------|------|
| Class of problem — general enough to recognise again | Task-specific names and identifiers |
| Invariant that was at risk | Commit hashes and session paths |
| What would have gone wrong without the fix | Step-by-step replay of this session |
| Corrections to prior entries | Anything that rotates per task |

Format: `<failing check> — <root cause> — <fix location>`

Write only after the fix passes. Update in place. Mark
superseded entries rather than deleting them.

---

## Standing orders

One sentence per exchange. Batch independent reads into one
call. Follow the documented interface. End on a committed
result, not a plan.
